card.title: Why Children Won't Stop Asking 'Why?'
card.teaser: By age 4, your kid will ask about 300 questions a day. It isn't a stall tactic — it's a tiny scientist at work.
card.coverImagePrompt: A wide-eyed preschooler holding a dandelion up to a grown-up, sunlit garden background, painterly, warm, full of wonder

lessons.title: The endless staircase of 'why'
lessons.body: Why is the sky blue? Because light scatters. Why does it scatter? Because of tiny molecules. Why are there tiny molecules? By question six, most adults wave a white flag. But the child isn't trolling you — they are running a remarkably efficient learning algorithm, and they are just getting started.

visual.imagePrompt: A chain of speech bubbles rising into a sunset sky, each with the word 'why?' in a child's handwriting

lessons.title: The question explosion
lessons.body: Between ages 2 and 5, children acquire roughly 10 new words a day. This is also the age when 'why?' erupts. Psycholinguist Michelle Chouinard found preschoolers ask about 76 information-seeking questions per hour with a familiar adult. Over a waking day, that can easily top 300 questions.

keyTerms.definition: A question asked because the child genuinely wants to know the answer
Understanding that other people know things you don't — and can tell you

visual.imagePrompt: An animated counter showing '300 questions / day' floating above a small child's silhouette

lessons.title: Why 'why?' is a power move
lessons.body: Before age 3, questions are mostly about facts ('What's that?'). Around 3, children start asking about causes — the structure of reality itself. This shift happens just as they're building mental models of how the world works. A 'why?' is essentially the child saying: *I have a gap in my model, and I'd like you to fill it.*

visual.imagePrompt: A cross-section of a child's head with interlocking gears labeled with concepts like gravity, kindness, weather

lessons.title: They know when you're dodging
lessons.body: Chouinard's transcripts showed something surprising: when adults gave a real explanation, kids moved on to the next topic. When adults dodged or gave a non-answer, the children asked again — often with the same word. They aren't repeating themselves out of habit; they're repeating themselves because the answer they got didn't actually solve anything.
lessons.funFact: In one study, children as young as 3 stopped asking follow-ups 78% of the time when an adult gave a causal explanation — versus only 37% when the answer was non-informative.

visual.imagePrompt: A child with a tilted head, intently listening to a grown-up, soft bokeh background, documentary feel

lessons.title: Answer like a scientist, not a judge
lessons.body: You don't need the perfect answer — you need an honest one. 'I don't know, let's find out' is a legitimate response and models real thinking. The goal isn't to satisfy every question; it's to keep the door to curiosity open. A kid who stops asking 'why?' has learned, somewhere, that asking isn't worth it.

quiz.question: Roughly how many questions per hour do preschoolers ask with a familiar adult?
quiz.explanation: Michelle Chouinard's research found preschoolers ask about 76 information-seeking questions per hour.

quiz.question: What changes about children's questions around age 3?
options: They ask in whole sentences for the first time
quiz.explanation: Around age 3, questions shift from labeling ('what's that?') to seeking causes and explanations.

quiz.question: What do kids typically do when adults give a non-informative answer?
options: They ask the same question again
quiz.explanation: Children persistently re-ask when an answer doesn't fill the gap — they know a dodge when they hear one.

sources.title: Children's questions: A mechanism for cognitive development
sources.publisher: Monographs of the Society for Research in Child Development

sources.title: The hungry mind: Intellectual curiosity is the third pillar of academic performance
